author: @SchwaAlien
text: I came across a salvaged Japanese PC fan that says it has a magnetic bearing, I wonder if it uses a similar configuration?  I stuck it into a case with a lithium ion cell to use as a small portable fan and it has been great for many years now, however it definitely has a little more whine sound when it runs and there is a noticeable clunk sound if you set it down hard when it’s off...  definitely an uncommon model to me, and I’ve salvaged many PC fans over the years...  seems like it may have been a premium part for equipment that needed to be more reliable for longer than the typical ball bearing fan, I can’t recall exactly where it was extracted from but I think it was just an office PC.

author: @billharm6006
text: Fun. However, scaling to meaningful power levels could prove problematic. Similar magnetically supported "desk toys" will start to bob after spinning for a while. The same type of bobbing or loping action has plagued some forms of maglev train projects. Magnetic bearings are, of course, available. Designs incorporating magnetic bearings are significantly different from this project, and, of course, have their own drawbacks and restrictions. Upshot: I wouldn't count on this concept as part of your "getting off the grid" project.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Induction coil and electromagnet.

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: Inductive heating and levitation. It's possible. Sometimes the technology is used in welding, especially automated welding - there's no contact between the workpiece and the welding machine, so the machine doesn't wear out. Takes a lot of power though.

replies.author: @arechikitsune
replies.text: thanks

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I once saw a frog being levitated

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering That was diamagnetic levitation. You can levitate a lot of things like that, including water - and frogs are mostly water. The effect is very weak though, so just overcoming gravity requires generating a magnetic field of immense strength. What you saw was a tiny frog, but you probably didn't see the room-sized stacked electromagnet assembly, or the adjacent rooms containing power supply and cooling equipment. It's difficult to even build an electromagnet so powerful that won't also tear itsself apart, and it needs constant cooling with refrigerated water just to keep from melting.


Graphite is much more strongly diamagnetic though, so it's possible - just barely - to float a sliver of that using equipment small enough to build at home.

author: @jasonwitt8619
text:
OH, so you understood exactly what I was talking about when I explained it. Yes that is what my project is except, the one I'm building is way bigger and strong, The magnets that it floats on, I'm using 1/2 inch thick 2 1/2 long N52's The strength of the magnets will be able to hold the weight of the generator wheel. My shaft is not going to touch anything, not even a glass and the wheel for the generator will be on the same shaft as the motor. The generator will be this. See link, oh and this generator can charge itself. Check this out. It is about the magnets doubled. Watch what happens when he adds to them. Add this muller setup generator in place of your fan, and bingo. 
Oh and keep in mind, the other coils you see on this wheel are 9 coils, they are turning the wheel but he unhooks one and shows you just what is possible. It will take double the amount of magnets but it will create way more energy per coil and this will be the generator that I will put on the same shaft as the motor. It will power itself.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CVT5ZleK5rY&t=71s

replies.author: @jasonwitt8619
replies.text: You are going to love this mate

replies.author: @jasonwitt8619
replies.text:
Oh and just a tip that I know about, on your coil, buy a couple of spools of magnetic copper wire. Also use a gas filled switch like the other video I showed you, the guy who was seeing 120 volts on his set up, on just one spool of magnetic wire, 22awg 
Here is a list of stuff you need in order to get the high volts using only one spool of wire.
All you need are these two and add them to your setup, and do what you said in your video Robert, put a nut in between your magnet and add more magnets two the one you already have.

Magnetic Quality REED Switches 2A 300V 36W 50mm glass
https://www.ebay.com/itm/252438120123#shpCntId

Amber Magnetic wire 200c - 22AWG  -  -  - Don't pick the green and orange, they can't stand the temperature, pick the copper looking one called "Amber"
Also you sit this whole spoil under the magnets with the above Reed hooked up. Like the other video I showed yesterday. He was getting some high RPM's on just two AA batteries
http://www.techfixx.com/


Oh and I'm using black boat board plastic to hold the magnets that are acting as a bearing. 1/2 inch thick, real strong stuff and slick.
https://www.boatoutfitters.com/cut-to-size-black-king-starboard

replies.author: @mwm2929
replies.text: I only always start with high voltage generators with low amps.  They are the easiest ones to find for free.  All you need is a shell type transformer to bring the voltage down and the amperage up.  Then a rectifier, capacitor, and voltage regulator for steady output.

author: @EyesOnReality
text: Hi Robert, great video as usual. You may be interested in what is called a Newman Motor, that is very much like your build now, with just an addition of an electromagnet powered by High Voltage pulses, being a high-torque ‘Voltage’ motor rather than the ever common ‘Current’ motor. Voltage is already in the atmosphere, as static electricity. Just adding an electromagnet (instead of wire windings) on top of your motor build with a commentator arrangement, you have a Newman motor. Search for “The Energy Machine of Joseph Newman’ for details. Happy Building.

LOOK  READ mr Smith you are truly blessed by God 

You are incredibly intelligent but there lots of highly intelligent people out there

You have a incredible attitude
Lots of people have a really good attitude 

You are incredibly creative 
Lot of creative people  out there

You have a incredible drive 
Lots of people have drive

Best of all you are humble 
Then again lots of humble people 

So what makes you
Blessed by God ?

What makes you one of the very few is you are all of these things in one package!
 
 I pray you are able to turn your brain off when you don't have a answer 

I pray when you don't have the answer you can still forget about it long enough to
 relax 

I pray you are not a expert in putting on a smile 

This is Ben Smith my wife and I been together for 24 years and married for 19 

She never writes on YouTube even though she enjoys watching your videos with me

You could  sell ice to Eskimos and make a entertaining video on how anyone can do it!
Funny  but so true lol

I use her youtube account I am very forgetful of small things like passwords

I write too much and you are very busy

Thanks for your encouragement

I have made some charcoal out of several different species of wood

Also about hour drive 800 acers of family property with lots of old coal mines 

Cant wait to go for a drive taking my multimeter with me

after carbonizing the different  wood only still chunks no other steps 8 mm some as low as 2 ohms lots under 20 ohms 

God bless you my friend !

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there are lots of reasons it wobbled a bit - it was made in four parts so each part wasn't identical - the field magnets are in slightly different vertical alignment so the field wasn't even - I could have taken  more time but this 8 min vid took all day to make and for me that was enough lol

replies.author: @SI-GOD
replies.text:
Love the work of N. Tesla. I would love to reproduce many osmf his experiments and try to bring back the wonderful things he invented/discovered before big money & government corruption stole it from the world.
But at the same time it scares me because I don't have all is the safety equipment to protect myself and my experience may not be limited to being thrown across the room with no apparent permanent damage to me. Also, various groups don't want this knowledge to be known and many of those who have tried to give it back to the world have disappeared or had weird 'freak accidents' taking their life. I'm ok with dying for the cause but only if I know it will get out to the public and not suppressed. Nothing to worry about yet since I have no way of doing experiments right now and may never have the huge gobs of money I need to start. Living paycheck-to almost paycheck really sucks.

replies.author: @SI-GOD
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I appreciate that response. The link does help.
I do vaguely know a little about whet goes into a video as I've tried to create some myself but is more tedious than I care to deal with. If you get that recording perfect the first time, you still have to cut the redundant parts and often splice the audio to match. The only video I actually managed to create was 6 minutes showing only my face and I had the script typed out. Took about 2 hours.
Once spent a week splicing a 12 minute guided meditation audio cutting out ALL of my tongue fumbles, adding silence time for visualizations, and so on. Never was completely happy with that one.
My "hats off" to all YouTube content producers. Keep up the good work and I look forward to learning much more from you.

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
I have used uPVC board for many of my projects. Rather than glueing the board together,I use the plastic blocks used to screw chip board furniture together. The board is interesting as it is available in a number of widths, some fitted with flange edges, making it ideal for building boxes or slopping panels. It is also available with slotted air vents along its length, making it suitable for equipment that requires air cooling. I have a chemical electric motor driven rocker box, used for developing PCBs, that is made from the stuff.

There are  large energy storage systems that use heavy rotating metal cylinders mounted in a vacuum to store energy to power telephone exchanges, during mains power failures. The cylinders act as motor/dynamos. To keep energy losses to a minimum, these use magnetic bearings to suspend the metal cylinders. The energy stored is so large, that the whole assemblies are usually buried underground in re-enforced concrete holes, just incase the rapidly rotating cylinder disintegrates.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: try the glue mate - it will surprise you at how strong the joint is and saves on blocks and screwing or bolting - you are spot on with he flywheel generators and this would make a good one for sure - you have just given me an idea lol - cheers

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I will consider using a combination of glue and blocks. I like to be able to take projects apart to service and sometimes to reuse and repurpose. One application I have found for the flanged board, is to use the flange to allow the board to be suspended below existing shelves, sliding down the gap between the shelf and the wall. This allow me to mount mains distribution blocks under the shelf for plugging in test equipment. It really is a very useful project building material.

replies.author: @goodtimeeric
replies.text: Thank you so much for that explanation. I remember as a toddler being puzzled by the phone working when nothing else would. You've made my day. :D

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
@goodtimeeric most exchanges use huge banks of batteries, to supply the phone network during mains outage. They float charge the batteries to deliver the -48 volts required by each connected telephone. As an engineering student, I was allowed access to my local exchange to see the hardware used. That was a very long time ago. At that time the batteries were lead acid, and the switching used were the mechanical strowger telephone relay selectors. The exchange had just begun replacing the mechanical system with TTL or Cmos logic.
The magnetic storage is reserved for big exchanges.
Since my student days, the telephone network has undergone several technological revolutions, converting  from switching analogue to digital audio.The standard  MSI (medium scale integration) logic has been replaced with dedicated PCM ( pulse code modulation) time space telephone switching. Then this was replaced with packet switching technology. This has now been replaced with systems compatible with the internet. 
I have been retired now for more than ten years, so I have lost track of the latest changes.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: @jasonwitt8619 No, You already have magnets pushing the shaft towards the end plate just stick another magnet on the end where the ballpoint is and then one on the endplate so they repel each other.
